step.title: Step 14
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 8 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 44, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a blue background with a light blue vertical divider strip, a yellow right-edge strip, and a black bottom bar. Three "F"-like shapes exist: (1) a black F-shape on the left containing 5 white dot children, (2) a charcoal F-shape on the right, and (3) a yellow F-shape at bottom-right. The white dots appear to be interactive elements. No clear player avatar visible.
- Goal model: Click on the F-shapes or white dots with MOUSE before the timer runs out. The white dots inside the black F-shape are likely targets.
- Action model: Arrow keys, SPACE, MOUSE, and ACTION7 available. Need to probe what they do.
- Plan: Move cursor left with many LEFT presses, then SPACE, to see if a mark appears away from the timer strip.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the F-shapes have moved to the center! The black F-shape is now on the left side of the divider and the charcoal F-shape is on the right side. They're both near the light blue divider strip. The image shows the F-shapes overlapping the divider area.
But wait, I notice the charcoal F-shape is no longer visible in the segmentation output! Let me check:
- After RIGHT x8, the segmentation shows:
  - Node 3: B, pixels=13 (timer)
  - Node 5: B, pixels=40 (black F-shape at cols 27-35)
  - Node 13: B, pixels=63 (bottom bar)
  - No charcoal F-shape (node 6) visible!
The charcoal F-shape disappeared! It must have moved into the timer strip or merged with something. Looking at the image, I can see the black F-shape near the divider, but the charcoal F-shape seems to have been absorbed or hidden.
Wait, looking at the image more carefully, I see the black F-shape with white dots on the LEFT side of the divider, and there's a dark shape on the RIGHT side that looks like part of the charcoal F-shape. But the segmentation doesn't show it separately.
Actually, looking at the image, the charcoal F-shape seems to have moved to the right side of the divider and might be overlapping with the timer strip. The timer strip is at column 63, and the charcoal F-shape was at cols 51-59. After 8 RIGHT actions, it would be at cols 51-24=27 to 59-24=35. Wait, that's the same as the black F-shape!
Hmm, let me recalculate:
- Black F was at cols 3-11, each RIGHT adds 3 → after 8 RIGHTs: cols 3+24=27 to 11+24=35
- Charcoal F was at cols 51-59, each RIGHT subtracts 3 → after 8 RIGHTs: cols 51-24=27 to 59-24=35
So both F-shapes are now at cols 27-35! They've overlapped! And since the divider is at cols 30-32, they're both centered on the divider.
The charcoal F-shape might have merged with the black F-shape or been hidden behind it. Let me check the segmentation more carefully.
Looking at the image, I can see:
- The black F-shape with white dots on the left side of the divider
- A dark shape (charcoal F) on the right side of the divider
- They seem to be partially overlapping the divider
This is interesting! The F-shapes have been brought together at the divider. Maybe this is the goal - to bring them to the center. Now I should try pressing SPACE to see if this completes the level.
But wait, the segmentation output only shows one F-shape (the black one). Let me check if the charcoal F merged or was absorbed. The image shows both shapes are still visible but they're overlapping at the divider.
Actually, looking at the segmentation output again:
```
B F: rows 0-23, cols 63-63
B F: rows 15-23, cols 27-35
```
The first one (rows 0-23, cols 63-63) is the timer strip. The second one (rows 15-23, cols 27-35) is the black F-shape. There's no separate charcoal F-shape node, which means it either merged with the black F or was absorbed.
Let me check the current segmentation more carefully to see if the charcoal F is now part of the black F or if it's hidden.
Actually, I think the important thing now is to test whether this configuration (F-shapes at the divider) is the goal. Let me press SPACE to see if it completes the level.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: The F-shapes are now at the divider (cols 27-35)! The charcoal F-shape seems to have merged. Let me check the full segmentation and try SPACE to test if this completes the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check full segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}..., children={n['children']}")

# Try SPACE to see if this completes the level
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE with F-shapes at divider - test level completion')
action(['SPACE'])

print(f"\nResult: {last_action_result}")
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print(f"\nNodes after SPACE: {len(seg2['nodes'])}")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
